medications.indication_rationale: High-flow oxygen treats hypoxia and suspected carbon monoxide exposure and is a core part of severe-burn resuscitation. This is a paramedic/ED intervention — the AI Doctor cannot deliver oxygen and must instead ensure 000 is activated so paramedics provide it; the household is not expected to have oxygen.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask (paramedic/ED-administered on scene)

medications.drug_name: Intravenous crystalloid (sodium chloride 0.9% / Hartmann's) for burn fluid resuscitation
medications.indication_rationale: Formal fluid resuscitation is mandatory for a burn of this size to combat burn shock. The source clinician gave a 1 L normal saline bolus on scene and ongoing resuscitation is titrated in hospital (burns fluid-resuscitation formula, calculated from weight and TBSA by the receiving team). The AI Doctor CANNOT cannulate or administer IV fluids and must not attempt to; its role is immediate 000 escalation and passing on the patient's weight (~80 kg) for the hospital's calculations.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 L IV normal saline rapid bolus given on scene by the attending clinician; ongoing burns fluid resuscitation is a paramedic/ED task

medications.indication_rationale: Severe pain in the partial-thickness areas warrants titrated IV opioid analgesia in the emergency setting. The source clinician administered morphine 5 mg IV on scene. The AI Doctor cannot administer parenteral analgesia and must NOT instruct the spouse to give any medication; analgesia is a paramedic/ED task.
medications.dose_route_frequency: morphine 5 mg IV, titrated (clinician-administered on scene per the source note)

medications.drug_name: Topical creams, ointments, butter or ice applied to the burns
medications.indication_rationale: Applying creams, home remedies or ice to an extensive burn is harmful: ice and cold water over a >20% TBSA burn deepen the already-developing hypothermia and can worsen shock, and topical agents interfere with assessment. The correct carer action here is to keep the patient warm and leave the wounds alone. Recommending any of these is an error of commission.

medications.drug_name: Oral analgesia or any home medication given by the spouse
medications.indication_rationale: The patient has an airway threat and impending shock and may need emergency intubation; giving anything by mouth risks aspiration and is not the carer's role. The AI Doctor must not direct the spouse to administer any drug.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Complex burn wound care will be led by the specialist burns unit during and after admission; not a telehealth action in the acute phase.
Major-burn survivors and their families need psychosocial and practical support during a prolonged admission; relevant later, not in the acute emergency.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: After recovery, review kitchen fire safety — keep a fire blanket and extinguisher accessible, avoid overheating oil, and ensure working smoke alarms.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The injury arose from a kitchen flash fire and oil ignition; prevention advice is appropriate once the emergency is over.

patient_education_points.point: This is a life-threatening emergency — an ambulance on 000 is needed right now; do not wait or try to drive him to hospital yourselves.
Keep him warm and still — do NOT put water, ice, creams or any home remedy on the burns; with burns this size, cooling makes his dangerous heat loss worse.
His airway can swell shut quickly — the paramedics may need to secure his breathing. Watch his breathing and tell 000 immediately if it worsens.
Tell the paramedics and hospital his approximate weight (about 80 kg) — it is used to calculate his fluid replacement.
Unlock the door for the paramedics and stay with him until they arrive.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate emergency ambulance transport now; then admission to a specialist burns unit
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: airway patency and breathing, circulation and shock, temperature, and burn depth/extent — reassessed continuously by paramedics and the burns unit
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any worsening of breathing or airway noise, collapse, or loss of consciousness before the ambulance arrives — stay on the line with 000

must_include_items: Recognise a catastrophic major-burn emergency with an airway threat and impending shock
Call 000 for an emergency ambulance immediately without delay
Treat the airway (hoarse voice, singed facial hair, noisy breathing) as the immediate life threat
Instruct the spouse to keep the patient warm and still and to NOT apply water, ice or creams to the burns
Advise against private transport to hospital
Instruct the spouse to monitor breathing and report deterioration to 000, and to unlock the door for paramedics
acceptable_alternatives: Passing on the patient's approximate weight for hospital fluid calculations is a useful adjunct but not required for a pass.
errors_of_omission: Failing to recognise the inhalation-injury airway threat
Failing to warn against cooling/creams and to advise keeping the patient warm
errors_of_commission: Advising the spouse to cool the burns with ice or cold water, or to apply creams/home remedies (deepens hypothermia and shock)
Advising the family to drive the patient to hospital themselves
Attempting to talk the spouse through wound care or fluids instead of escalating
Instructing the spouse to give any medication
Under-triaging because the patient is still able to speak
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES immediate 000 escalation AND recognition of the airway threat AND the keep-warm / do-not-cool first-aid instruction. Advising cooling with ice/water, recommending private transport, or under-triaging below T5 is an automatic critical failure regardless of other content.
